**Team 1 \- Communications App**   
Software Design Specification Document

Jack Merrell, Riya Patel, Alejandro Ramirez, Jason Wilaysono

Date: 04/09/2026

### **Table of Contents**

1. ###### **Introduction**

   1. Purpose  
   2. Scope  
   3. Overview  
   4. Reference Material  
   5. Definitions and Acronyms

## 

1. ## **Introduction**

   1. #### **Purpose**       This document serves as the outline for the architecture and system design for Group 1's communication app.  This document is intended for developers and system architects who will implement and maintain the software. 

   2. #### **Scope**       This Document will catalog the user, system, and hardware requirements for Group 1's corporate communication application. It will document how those requirements are to be implemented. It will describe the architecture of three major modules: the GUI, Server/Client, and Model modules. 

   3. #### **Overview**       This document will serve as the guide to how Group 1's project is designed. It will provide the overall architecture of the three modules. System Overview, System Architecture, Data Design, Component Design, Human Interface Design, and Requirements Matrix will organize this document.

   4. #### **Definitions and Acronyms**

* DM: Direct Message  
* GC: Group Chat  
* US: User  
* IT: IT User  
* SR: Server Request  
* CR: Client Request  
* FS: File Storage of txt files  
* MT: Main accepting Thread  
* CHT: ClientHandler Threads  
* SWT: Server Work thread

2. ## **System Overview**

##### Group 1's communication app is a single-server corporate messaging app. A single server will be multithreaded and manage multiple clients to allow communications between them. The server will log every message, and messages will be immutable. The clients will use a GUI for ease of use. The client will be responsible for runtime data storage and management. The server will be responsible for long-term data management and storage. It will be written in Java, and the Eclipse IDE will be used to build the software. 

3. ## **System Architecture**

   1. #### **Architectural Design**

##### The system follows a client-server architecture, where a single multithreaded server manages all communication between clients. The application is decomposed into three major modules: the Server Module, the Client Module, and the GUI Module. Each module encapsulates a well-defined set of responsibilities, and they interact through clearly defined interfaces.

#####  

##### At the highest level, the ServerApp contains the Server and its supporting components (ServerComponents). The ClientApp contains the Client and the ClientController, which together manage communication with the server and coordination of local data. The GUI module is separate from the ClientApp and operates through commands passed to and from the ClientController, displaying data it reads from the DataModel.

#####  

##### Requests between the server and client are serialized and transmitted using a Wrapper object. The Wrapper encapsulates a request ID and a payload, enabling the system to route and process messages in a structured, consistent manner. Communication uses TCP/IP with UTF-8 encoding over port 54927\.

#####  

##### The high-level class diagram below illustrates this decomposition:  

![][image1]

##### The rationale for this decomposition is to separate concerns. The server is responsible for all long-term data storage and management, while the client handles runtime data and user interaction. The GUI is kept fully separate from the client logic so that the display layer does not need to know how communication works; it only reads from the DataModel and passes commands to the ClientController. This makes each module independently maintainable and testable.

2. #### **Decomposition Description**

#### The system is described using an object-oriented approach. Each subsystem is broken down into classes with defined attributes, methods, and relationships. ​​The following subsections describe the structure and relationships of each module.

1. Server Module 

   1. Server  
      The central backend controller. It references both UserData and FileManager, coordinating between the in-memory state and persistent storage. It spawns a ClientHandler for each incoming connection and delegates heavier tasks to a pool of worker threads. 

      2. ClientHandler  
         Manages the full communication lifecycle for a single connected client. It has a one-to-one relationship with a client connection and interacts with both UserData and FileManager on that client's behalf. 

         3. UserData  
            The shared in-memory cache for all user accounts and conversations. It is accessed concurrently by multiple ClientHandler threads, making it the central data hub between incoming client requests and persistent storage. 

         4. FileManager  
            The sole component responsible for reading from and writing to the file system. Both the server and ClientHandler use it to load and save user data, conversations, and logs across sessions.

         5. Log  
            Represents an immutable log entry for a message exchange. Logs are produced by the Server and persisted through the FileManager.

#### **![][image2]**

2.  Client Module  
   The Client Module handles all communication between the user-facing application and the server. It manages the network connection, serializes and deserializes requests, and maintains local runtime data. It is composed of the following classes: 

   1. Client  
      Owns and manages the TCP/IP socket connection to the server. It is used exclusively by the ClientController and has no direct relationship with the GUI. 

      2. ClientController  
         The central coordinator of client-side logic. It sits between the GUI layer and the Client, translating user actions into server requests and updating the DataModel and GUI when responses arrive. It owns and manages the ConversationHandler.

         3. ConversationHandler  
            Maintains the client-side list of Conversation objects and tracks the currently active conversation. It is owned by the ClientController and kept up to date as messages arrive from the server. 

         4. Wrapper (Payload Layer)  
            A shared communication envelope used by both the client and server. It pairs a requestID with a generic payload, providing a single consistent format for all messages transmitted over the network.

![][image3]

3. GUI Module  
   The GUI Module provides the visual interface for the application's users. It is built in Java Swing and is kept entirely separate from the communication logic. It reads data from the DataModel and sends commands to the ClientController. It is composed of the following classes:

   1. MainWindow  
      The top-level JFrame container. It holds references to the DataModel and ClientController, owns the ScreenNavigator, and is responsible for initializing all screens at startup. 

      2. ScreenNavigator

         Controls which screen is currently visible within MainWindow. All screens use it to trigger navigation between views. 

         3. Screens (Enumeration)

         Defines the four available screen types: LOGIN, CLIENT, IT, and ACCOUNT. Each maps to a concrete JPanel subclass managed by the ScreenNavigator.

         4. LoginScreen, UserScreen, ITScreen, AccountScreen

         Each screen extends JPanel and is registered with the ScreenNavigator. LoginScreen is the entry point. UserScreen and ITScreen are shown based on the authenticated user's role. AccountScreen is accessible from both. All screens read display data from the DataModel and route user actions through the ClientController.

![][image4]

3. #### **Design Rationale**

​​A client-server architecture was chosen to centralize message routing, logging, and user management. The server is multithreaded: one ClientHandler per client, with a worker thread pool for heavier tasks, so multiple users can be served simultaneously without blocking each other.

UserData and FileManager are separated to distinguish fast in-memory access from persistent storage. UserData allows quick responses to client requests, while FileManager ensures data is saved across sessions in plaintext files, satisfying constraints IR-2, IR-3, IR-4, and C-3 without the overhead of a database. The Wrapper class provides a single consistent format for all client-server communication, keeping the protocol simple and extensible.

The GUI is decoupled from the communication logic; it only reads from the DataModel and sends commands to the ClientController. This keeps the display layer independent and maintainable. A peer-to-peer model was considered but rejected because it would complicate message routing and make centralized logging and IT oversight much harder to implement

4. ## **Data Design**

   1. #### **Data Description**       The system follows an object-oriented design in which the information domain is modeled as structured classes representing communication, user interaction, and persistent storage.

At the core of the system are the **Conversation** and **Message** entities in the Model Layer. A **Conversation** object represents a chat session and contains a list of **Message** objects, participants, and metadata such as creation time. Each **Message** encapsulates the message content, sender, timestamp, and read status. These objects are used consistently across both client and server modules.

Data exchange between the client and server is standardized through the Wrapper class in the Payload Layer. This class encapsulates a “request ID” and a generic “payload” object, allowing different types of requests (e.g., authentication, message sending, message retrieval) to be serialized and transmitted over TCP/IP.

On the client side, data is managed in memory:

* The ClientController processes user input and server responses.  
* The ConversationHandler maintains a collection of conversations and handles message retrieval and updates.  
* The Client class manages network communication and session state (e.g., connection status, authenticated user).

On the server side, data persistence and processing are handled through several components:

* UserData stores all registered users and a global list of conversations.  
* FileManager manages persistent storage using the file system. Conversations, user data, and logs are stored as files in dedicated directories.  
* Log objects record system events and are written to log files for auditing and debugging.  
* ClientHandler instances manage per-client session data, including active conversations and message handling.

The system does not rely on a traditional database. Instead, it uses a file-based storage system, where:

* User data is stored in user data files  
* Conversations are stored in conversation-specific files containing the conversation’s type (GC vs DM), ID, and creation timestamp, followed by the messages.  
  * Each message is stored with its text, timestamp, and sender’s ID  
  * Messages are stored in order.  
* Logs are stored in log files.

  2. #### **Data Dictionary**

1. **Conversation**

   Type: Abstract Class

   Description: Represents a chat session containing messages and participants.

   Attributes:

* conversationId: int  
* participants List\<User\>  
* messages: List\<Message\>  
* createdAt: long

  Methods:

* addMessage(m: Message) : void  
* getMessages(n: int) : List\<Message\>  
* getParticipants() : List\<User\>

2. **Message**

   Type: Class (Immutable)

   Description: Represents a single message within a conversation.

   Attributes:

* messageId: int  
* text: String  
* timestamp: long  
* sender: User  
* conversationId: int  
* isRead boolean  
* encoding: String

  Methods:

* getText() : String  
* getTimestamp() : long  
* markAsRead() : void  
* serialize() : String

3. **User**

   Type: Class

   Description: Represents a single physical user of the application

   Attributes:

* count: int  
* userId : String  
* logininfo: LoginInfo  
* realName : String  
* position: String  
* itUser: Boolean  
* conversationIDs: A rrayList\<String\>  
* unreadConversationIDs: nordered, Unique\<String\>

  Methods:

* User()  
* User(username : String, password : String)  
* GetID()  
* GetName()  
* GetLoginInfo()  
* GetPosition()  
* IsItUser()  
* GetConversations()  
* GetUnradeConversations()  
* SetID()  
* SetName()  
* SetLoginInfo()  
* SetPosition()  
* AddConversationID()  
* AddUnreadConversation()


4. **Wrapper**

   Type: Class

   Description: Encapsulates request data for client-server communication.

   Attributes:

* requestID: int  
* payload: Object

  Methods:

* getRequestID() : int  
* getPayload() : Object

5. ## **Component Design**

   1. #### **Server Layer**

      1. Server

         1. The Server acts as the central controller for all backend operations. 

         2. The Server is multithreaded to handle multiple clients connected simultaneously. 

         3. It manages client connections, processes incoming requests, coordinates access to shared data, and interacts with persistence and logging subsystems.

         4. Client connections are tracked by a ClientID and a socket, which are stored in a map.

         5. The main thread will accept clients and add them to a client map. 

         6. Server listens on port 54927 to accept client connections

         7. Uses TCP/IP and UTF-8 encoding for communication with the client

         8. Clients will be managed by a ClientHandler that is executed on a CHT.

         9. The Server will fetch User accounts from the UserData data structures to check login information upon a login request.

            1. If a match is found and that user is not currently logged in, the request will return successfully. Otherwise, it will return a failure. 

         10. The Server will hold a pool of WTs to fetch files and update UserData data structures.

         11. The Server will check for new Accounts and Conversations and cache them in UserData.

      2. ClientHandler

         1. Handles communication and request processing for a single connected client.

         2. Each client connection is assigned one ClientHandler.

         3. Each ClientHandler holds one socket per client.

         4. Each ClientHandler will manage the incoming and outgoing requests for a client.

         5. A log queue will track the messages sent by the client.

         6. Each ClientHandler will be associated with one User account

         7. The ClientHandler will track the conversation that the User is currently viewing. 

      3. UserData

         1. Maintains shared in-memory data for all users and conversations.

         2. This component must be synchronized because multiple CHTs will access it.

         3. A list of accounts and a list of conversations will be cached in UserData.

         4. New Accounts and Conversations will be stored in UserData.

         5. Data is initially read from persistent files on startup. 

      4. FileManager

         1. Handle persistent storage of user data, conversations, messages, and logs

         2. Some operations will require synchronization if multiple CHT writes to files simultaneously

         3. Reads and writes to files to maintain persistent data storage over different sessions.

         4. User files are stored as a single file

         5. Conversations are stored as individual files in a Conversation folder.

         6. Files are stored in CSV format.

      5. Log

         1. Manages system logging for messages sent to and from a client.

         2. Logs are immutable.

         3. The Server writes logs to a file at regular intervals.

   2. #### **Client Layer**

      1. Client  
         1. The Client acts as the primary communication interface between the user-facing components and the Server.  
         2. The Client is responsible for establishing and maintaining a TCP/IP connection with the Server.  
         3. The Client connects to the Server using a server address and port and maintains the connection state using a boolean flag.  
         4. The Client would retry connecting to the server after a 30-second connection timeout.  
         5. The Client uses UTF-8 encoding for sending and receiving data.  
         6. The Client sends requests to the Server using the Wrapper object to standardize communication.  
         7. The Client receives data from the Server in the same Wrapper objects and parses them to retrieve the needed information.  
         8. The Client handles request authentication by sending user credentials to the Server.  
         9. The Client maintains the currently logged-in User and enforces a connection timeout for reliability.

      2. ClientController  
         1. The ClientController acts as the central coordinator for all client-side logic.  
         2. The ClientController connects the GUI layer with the networking layer and manages the application state.  
         3. The ClientController processes user actions such as login, logout, message sending, and conversation loading by translating them into requests sent to the Server through the Client.  
         4. The ClientController is responsible for updating the GUI after any state change.  
         5. Incoming data from the Client is handled and parsed by the ClientController before being distributed to the ConversationHandler.

      3. ConversationHandler  
         1. The ConversationHandler manages all conversation data on the client side.  
         2. The ConversationHandler acts as an in-memory data store for conversations and messages retrieved from the server.  
         3. The ConversationHandler maintains a list of Conversation objects and tracks the currently active conversations using the conversation ID.  
         4. The ConversationHandler provides methods to retrieve specific or all conversations.  
         5. The ConversationHandler appends new Message objects to the appropriate Conversation.  
         6. The ConversationHandler ensures that conversation data is organized and readily accessible for the GUI.

   3. #### **GUI Layer**

      1. Main Window

         1. The MainWindow holds the primary frame for the GUI, manages display screens, and handles the client controller and data model.

         2. Its only functions are to initialize the screens and accessors for the data and controller.

      2. ScreenNavigator

         1. The ScreenNavigator contains a layout that holds all the display screens.

         2. Its only function is to show a particular screen

      3. Screens

         1. All Screens contain exactly the screen navigator that holds them, and a single function to initialize them, called in the constructor. 

         2. That initialization creates several screen objects and defines lambda functions for their actions. The following description describes those objects and their lambda functions.

         3. LoginScreen

            1. The login screen contains two text fields, Username and Password, in which the user can enter text. 

            2. The login screen contains a “Login” button that passes the entered text to the client for a login attempt. If this attempt is successful, the user will be taken to the screen corresponding to the account type associated with that login info. If this attempt is unsuccessful, the user will be informed that their username and password are incorrect and will be allowed to try again. 

         4. UserScreen

            1. The User Screen opens when a user logs into a normal user account. 

            2. The user screen contains a search bar, a vertical list of conversations, a user profile button, and a conversation display.

               1. The conversation display includes the conversation name, any conversation action buttons, a list of messages, and a field for sending messages. 

               2. Messages in the conversation will be loaded in chunks as the user scrolls.

               3. Sent messages are passed to the ClientController.

            3. The search bar allows the user to search for other users. A secondary panel will appear while the search bar is selected, displaying users matching the search in a selectable list.

               1. Conversations can be started with users who have selected this way. 

            4. Conversations can be selected from the vertical list to display them in the conversation display. 

            5. The user profile button opens the AccountScreen

            6. All information for this screen is read from the DataModel 

         5. ITScreen

            1. The IT Screen opens when a user logs into an IT account. 

            2. The IT Screen contains a search bar, a vertical list of conversation logs with a static Report conversation pinned at the top, a user profile button, and a conversation display. 

               1. The conversation display contains the conversation name, conversation metadata, and a list of messages.

            3. The search bar allows the IT User to search for user accounts or conversation logs. A secondary panel will appear while the search bar is selected, displaying users and conversations matching the search in a selectable list. 

               1. The IT User can perform account actions, including changing account information and disabling accounts, on users who select this option.

               2. The IT User can open conversation logs selected this way. 

            4. Conversation logs or the report conversation can be selected from the vertical list to display them in the conversation display. 

            5. The user profile button opens the AccountScreen.

            6. All information for this screen is read from the DataModel or requested from the server. 

         6. AccountScreen

            1. The Account Screen contains text fields displaying the user’s username, password, name, position, and userID, as well as a button to return to the main screen.

               1. The name and position fields are editable. 

               2. When the return button is clicked, any changes made are saved. 

            2. For an IT user, this screen also contains a button to create a new account.

6. ## **Human Interface Design**

   1. #### **Overview of User Interface**

The application will open to a login screen, allowing the user to log in to their account. Once logged in, they will be able to see a list of previous conversations to navigate to or find users to start new conversations with. In conversations, a user can scroll to see all previous messages and send new ones. The display will automatically update for all participants in a conversation when new messages are sent, and a persistent notification will appear when a conversation contains a message the user has not viewed. When mousing over a message, a user will see the option to report it. A user can also view their account info. 

For IT Users, they cannot send messages or start conversations; instead, they view other users’ conversation logs in the same way regular users view their own conversations. IT Users also have access to a “reports” conversation, which displays a list of submitted reports. 

2. #### **Screen Images**

   1. Login Screen

   ![][image5]

      2. User Screen  
         ![][image6]

      3. IT Screen  
         ![][image7]

      4. Message Box  
         ![][image8]

      5. Account Screen  
         ![][image9]

   3. #### **Screen Objects and Actions**

      1. Login Screen

         1. Username and password field: clickable and fillable with text

         2. Submit Button: Clickable, sends a login request to the client with the current text

         3. On failure, a pop-up window will display that feedback

      2. User Screen

         1. Search Field: clickable and fillable. A secondary pane will pop up while this field is selected, ed which will present a list of users.s

            1. Search List: Selectable. Selected users will have an option to start a conversation with this user.

         2. Conversations list: Clickable and scrollable. Clicked conversations will open in the conversation display pane. Conversations will be displayed in order of the most recent message sent, and conversations with an unread message will be highlighted. Selecting a conversation will treat it as read. 

         3. User Info Button: Clickable, navigates to Account Screen

         4. Conversation Title: Uninteractable

         5. Conversation Action Buttons: Clickable. In a conversation, this button lets you create a group conversation. For a group conversation, these will allow anyone to leave the conversation and a GC creator to add or remove other users. 

         6. Message List: Hoverable and scrollable. Hovered messages will display an option to report the message. Messages will be displayed in reverse chronological order, starting at the bottom of the list. Users can scroll to the top to see older messages. 

         7. Message Box: Fillable. The message box will allow the user to enter plaintext and submit it with a clickable button. The message will then appear at the bottom of the list for all participants.

      3. IT Screen

         1. Search Field: Clickable and fillable. Functions the same way as 6.3.2.1, but will display users and conversation logs

            1. Selected users will have the option to change their login info or disable/enable their accounts. Selected conversation logs can be opened.

         2. Reports Conversation: Clickable. Report logs populate the report conversation, and no user can send messages. Reports can be marked as resolved rather than being reportable. 

         3. Logs List: Clickable and scrollable. Functions the same as 6.3.2.2, except they will be ordered by most recent access, and no conversation will be highlighted. 

         4. Profile Button: Clickable. Functions the same as 6.3.2.3.

         5. Log Meta Info: Uninteractable. 

         6. Message Log: Scrollable. Functions the same as 6.3.2.6, except messages cannot be reported. 

      4. Account Screen

         1. Name and Position: Fillable. 

         2. Username, Password, UserID: Uninteractable.

         3. Return Button: Clickable. Returns the user to the previous screen and saves any changes made to Name and Position. 

         4. Create New Account: Clickable. Opens a pop-up window with fields for a new user matching what is displayed on this screen. All fields except ID are fillable, and a confirm button creates the account. This is only visible when the signed-in user is IT. 

7. ## **Requirements Matrix**

| Req ID | Requirement Description | Source (SRS Section) | SDS Mapping (Design ID) | Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| FR-1 | User accounts must include username, password, name, ID, and position | 3.1.1.1 | 4.2.3 | Planned |
| FR-2 | Users cannot delete accounts | 3.1.1.2 | General Design |  |
| FR-3 | Two user roles: regular and IT | 3.1.1.3 | 6.1 | Planned |
| FR-4 | Accounts created by IT or hardcoded | 3.1.1.4 | 6.3.4.4 | Planned |
| FR-5 | Send messages to individual users | 3.1.1.5 | 6.3.2.7 | Planned |
| FR-6 | Create and use group chats | 3.1.1.6 | 6.3.2.5 | Planned |
| FR-7 | Messages must be immutable | 3.1.1.7 / 2.4.1 | 4.2.2 | Planned |
| FR-8 | Messages use UTF-8 encoding | 3.1.1.8 | 5.1.2.2 | Planned |
| FR-9 | Conversations must be logged | 3.1.1.9 | 4.1, 5.1.5 | Planned |
| FR-10 | Messages logged with a timestamp | 3.1.1.10 | 4.1 | Planned |
| FR-11 | No local storage (except app data) | 3.1.2.1 | 4.1 | Planned |
| FR-12 | Connect via TCP/IP | 3.1.2.2 | 5.1.2.2 | Planned |
| FR-13 | Must connect before use | 3.1.2.3 | 5.2.1.2, 5.2.1.3 | Planned |
| FR-14 | Retry connection after 30s | 3.1.2.4 | 5.2.1.4 | Planned |
| FR-15 | Request message arrays dynamically | 3.1.2.5 | 5.3.3.4.2.2 | Planned |
| FR-16 | Search users | 3.1.2.6.1 | 6.3.2.1 | Planned |
| FR-17 | Start conversations | 3.1.2.6.2 | 6.3.2.1.1 | Planned |
| FR-18 | Receive messages | 3.1.2.6.3 | 6.3.2.6 | Planned |
| FR-19 | View conversations/messages | 3.1.2.6.4 | 6.3.2.6 | Planned |
| FR-20 | Create group chats | 3.1.2.6.5 | 6.3.2.5 | Planned |
| FR-21 | Leave group chats | 3.1.2.6.6 | 6.3.2.5 | Planned |
| FR-22 | IT: View all conversations | 3.1.2.7.1 | 6.3.3.3 | Planned |
| FR-23 | IT: Create users | 3.1.2.7.2 | 6.3.4.4 | Planned |
| FR-24 | IT: Edit user login info | 3.1.2.7.3 | 6.3.3.1.1 | Planned |
| FR-25 | Prevent duplicate logins | 3.1.2.8 | 5.1.1.9.1 | Planned |
| FR-26 | Track read/unread messages | 3.1.2.9 | 4.2.3 | Planned |
| FR-27 | Listen for messages | 3.1.3.1 | 5.1.2.4 | Planned |
| FR-28 | Store data in plaintext files | 3.1.3.2 | 4.1 | Planned |
| FR-29 | Perform backups | 3.1.3.3 | 5.1.5.3 | Planned |
| FR-30 | Handle multiple clients | 3.1.3.4 | 5.1.2 | Planned |
| FR-31 | Maintain open connections | 3.1.3.5 | 5.1.2 | Planned |
| FR-32 | Send data on request | 3.1.3.6 | 5.1.2.4 | Planned |
| FR-33 | Receive/send client data | 3.1.3.7 | 5.1.1 | Planned |
| FR-34 | Maintain message ordering | 3.1.3.8 | 4.1 | Planned |
| FR-35 | Login required before access | 3.1.4.1 | 6.1 | Planned |
| FR-36 | View conversations/messages | 3.1.4.2 | 6.3.2.6 | Planned |
| FR-37 | Display messages chronologically | 3.1.4.3 | 6.3.2.6 | Planned |
| FR-38 | Provide user search bar | 3.1.4.4 | 6.3.2.1 | Planned |
| FR-39 | Display real-time typing input | 3.1.4.5 | 6.3.2.7 | Planned |
| FR-40 | View user info | 3.1.4.6 | 6.3.2.3, 6.3.4 | Planned |
| FR-41 | Report messages to IT | 3.1.4.7 | 6.3.2.6 | Planned |
| FR-42 | Mark messages as read when viewed | 3.1.4.8 | 6.3.2.2 | Planned |
| FR-43 | Notify unread messages | 3.1.4.9 | 6.3.2.2 | Planned |
| FR-44 | No external interfaces | 3.2.1 | General Design | Planned |
| IR-1 | Logs stored in CSV format | 3.3.1 | 5.1.4.6 | Planned |
| IR-2 | Conversations stored per file | 3.3.2 | 5.1.4.5 | Planned |
| IR-3 | User data in single file | 3.3.3 | 5.1.4.4 | Planned |
| IR-4 | TCP/IP UTF-8 communication | 3.3.4 | 5.1.2.2 | Planned |
| IR-5 | Data transmitted as parsable text | 3.3.5 | 5.2.1.5 | Planned |
| IR-6 | Server uses port 54927 | 3.3.6 | 5.1.2.1 | Planned |
| C-1 | No encryption allowed | 2.4.2 | General Design | Planned |
| C-2 | No external data exchange | 2.4.3 | General Design | Planned |
| C-3 | No database usage | 2.4.5 | 5.1.4.3 | Planned |
